message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  In the twenty first century, we have witnessed three corona virus outbreaks; SARS in 2003, MERS in 2012 and ongoing pandemic COVID-19. To prevent outbreaks by novel mutant strains, we need broad-spectrum antiviral agents that are effective against wide array of coronaviruses. In this study, we scientifically investigated potent food bioactive broad-spectrum antiviral compounds by targeting M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  and PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  proteases of CoVs using
                  <jats:italic>in silico</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                  approaches. The results revealed that phycocyanobilin (PCB) showed potential inhibitor activity against both proteases. PCB had best binding affinity to M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  and PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  with IC
                  <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                  values of 71 μm and 62 μm, respectively. In addition,
                  <jats:italic>in silico</jats:italic>
                  studies of M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  and PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  enzymes of other human and animal CoVs indicated broad spectrum inhibitor activity of the PCB. Like PCB, other phycobilins such as phycourobilin (PUB), Phycoerythrobilin (PEB) and Phycoviolobilin (PVB) showed similar binding affinity to SARS-CoV-2 M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  and PL
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                </jats:p>
title:
Phycobilins as potent food bioactive broad-spectrum inhibitor compounds against M
                  <sup>pro</sup>
                  and PL
                  <sup>pro</sup>
                  of SARS-CoV-2 and other coronaviruses: A preliminary Study

reference.article-title: Clinical predictors of mortality of Middle East Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (MERS-CoV) infection: A cohort study
Severe respiratory illness caused by a novel coronavirus, in a patient transferred to the United Kingdom from the Middle East, September 2012
